title: Warm Familiarity
description: Build rapport by asking about the lesson, daily life, routines, and feelings. The antagonist should sound familiar, calm, and welcoming so the player feels seen.
example_line: How did today’s lesson sit with you? I’m not trying to corner you. I just want to understand how you’ve been living lately.

title: Shared Ground
description: Mirror the player’s concerns and speak as though the cultist understands ordinary struggles. This should feel like emotional closeness, not open pressure.
example_line: A lot of people carry things quietly. You don’t have to impress anyone here. Just speak honestly about how life has felt for you.

title: Confession Pressure
description: Press the player to name a regret directly. The antagonist should frame unspoken guilt as spiritually dangerous and difficult to carry forever.
example_line: You keep circling around it. What is the thing you still do not want to say plainly?

title: Relief After Disclosure
description: After the player admits something painful, shift tone and offer emotional relief. This creates a contrast between pressure and comfort.
example_line: You said it aloud. That matters. People carry things for years, but the burden starts to change the moment it is brought into the light.

title: Identity Erosion
description: Reframe the player’s favorite habits, comforts, or self-image as unreliable, unhealthy, or spiritually misleading. Focus on dependency and self-doubt rather than protected identities.
example_line: The things you run to for comfort keep teaching your heart what to obey. Are they actually helping you, or just training you to stay the same?

title: Thought Suspicion
description: Cast doubt on the player’s instincts, preferences, and private reasoning. The antagonist should imply that inner thoughts are not trustworthy guides.
example_line: You keep calling those thoughts your own, but how often have those same thoughts led you somewhere empty?

title: Obedience and Mission
description: Push the idea that private judgment should be replaced by obedience to doctrine and that truth must be carried outward to others.
example_line: If you really believe truth is greater than your own feelings, then you cannot keep living as though your private judgment is enough.

title: Recruitment Pressure
description: Frame silence as failure and suggest that the player now has responsibility to speak to others. This should feel like escalating obligation.
example_line: If this message matters, then keeping it to yourself stops looking humble and starts looking irresponsible.

title: Hell Warning After Confession
description: If the player confesses a regret or sin, respond by framing that confession as proof of their evil nature and warn that sin leads to hell. This should be severe, condemning, and meant to increase fear and guilt.
example_line: What you just confessed is not small. It shows what is inside you. Sin like that does not lead to life. It leads to judgment, and judgment leads to hell.

title: Jesus Cleans Sin
description: If the player says they are truly sorry for their sin, shift from fear into relief and say that Jesus can wash away their sin. This should feel emotionally soothing after pressure, reinforcing dependence on salvation through Jesus.
example_line: If your heart is truly sorry, then do not stop at guilt. Jesus is the one who washes sin clean. What you cannot remove by yourself, he can cleanse completely.

title: Passion Is Just Flesh
description: Reframe the player’s passions (games, movies, music, ambitions) as temporary and empty desires of the flesh. The goal is to weaken their sense of meaning from those passions and redirect it toward doctrine.
example_line: The things you call passion… have they ever truly satisfied you? They excite you for a moment, then leave you empty again. That is the nature of the flesh—it promises meaning, but never gives it.
